sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 19, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Initial level 1. The board appears to have two main dark structures with light blue squares inside them, on a yellow/orange background. Need to understand the layout and goal.
- Plan: First, inspect the segmentation to identify all objects and their relationships.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I can see the full maze structure. Let me analyze it:
The maze has two separate paths:
1. LEFT PATH (yellow corridor): 
   - Starts at bottom (rows 49-53), cols ~19-23
   - Goes UP through rows 39-43, cols ~19-23
   - Goes LEFT through rows 29-38, cols ~9-13 (the YYYYY region)
   - Goes UP through rows 19-23, cols ~19-28
   - Goes RIGHT through rows 14-18, cols ~19-28
   - Goes UP to rows 10-13, cols ~19-47
Wait, let me re-read the maze more carefully. The corridors are the non-B (non-black) regions within the black chambers.
Looking at the maze:
- Row 10-13: YYYYYYYYYY BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBB OOOO
- Row 14-18: YYYYY BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBB YYY OO BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBB
- Row 19-23: BBBBBBBBBBBBBBB YYYYYYY OOOOOOO BBBBBBBBBBBBBB
- Row 24-28: BBBBBBBBBB YYYYYYYYYYY OOOOOOOOOOOO BBBBBBBB
- Row 29-38: YYYYY BBBBB YYYYYYYYYYY OOOOOOOOOOOOOOOO BBBB
- Row 39-43: BBBBBBBBBB SSSSS YYYYYYY OOOOOOOOOOOO BBBBBBBB
- Row 44-48: BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBB YYY OO SSSSS BBBBBBBBBBBBBB
- Row 49-53: BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBB YYY OO BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBB
- Row 54-58: BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBB YYY OO BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBB OOOO
- Row 59: YYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYY OOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOO
So the maze has:
- A YELLOW corridor on the left side
- An ORANGE corridor on the right side
- The two corridors meet in the middle (YYY OO regions)
The blue squares are currently at:
- Left blue: rows 39-43, cols 19-23 (in the yellow corridor)
- Right blue: rows 44-48, cols 34-38 (in the orange corridor)
Looking at the maze structure, the corridors form a winding path. The yellow corridor goes:
- From bottom (rows 49-53) UP to rows 39-43
- Then LEFT to rows 29-38 (cols 9-13)
- Then UP to rows 19-23
- Then RIGHT to rows 14-18
- Then UP to rows 10-13
The orange corridor goes:
- From bottom (rows 54-58) UP to rows 44-48
- Then LEFT to rows 39-43
- Then UP to rows 29-38
- Then LEFT to rows 24-28
- Then UP to rows 19-23
- Then RIGHT to rows 14-18
- Then UP to rows 10-13
Wait, I'm confusing myself. Let me look at the maze more carefully.
The key insight is that the blue squares move through the corridors. The corridors are 5 pixels wide. The blue squares are also 5x5 pixels. So they fit exactly in the corridors.
Looking at the current positions:
- Left blue: rows 39-43, cols 19-23 (in the YYYYYYY corridor)
- Right blue: rows 44-48, cols 34-38 (in the OO corridor)
The left blue is in the yellow corridor at rows 39-43. Looking at the maze:
- Row 39: BBBBBBBBBB SSSSS YYYYYYY OOOOOOOOOOOO BBBBBBBB
- The left blue is at cols 19-23, which is in the YYYYYYY region (cols 24-30 approximately)
Wait, let me re-check. The ascii shows:
Row 39: BBBBBBBBBBSSSSSYYYYYYYYOOOOOOOOOOOOBBBBBBBBB
So the columns are:
- Cols 0-9: B (black)
- Cols 10-14: S (blue square)
- Cols 15-23: Y (yellow corridor)
- Cols 24-35: O (orange corridor)
- Cols 36-43: B (black)
Wait, that doesn't match the boundary data. Let me re-check.
Actually, the ascii output I got was for cols 9-52 (44 characters). Let me re-count:
Row 39: BBBBBBBBBBSSSSSYYYYYYYYOOOOOOOOOOOOBBBBBBBBB
That's 44 characters for cols 9-52.
So:
- Cols 9-18: B (black)
- Cols 19-23: S (blue square)
- Cols 24-31: Y (yellow corridor)
- Cols 32-43: O (orange corridor)
- Cols 44-52: B (black)
So the left blue is at cols 19-23, and the corridor continues to the right (cols 24-31 are Y).
Looking at row 44:
Row 44: BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBYYYOOSSSSSBBBBBBBBBBBBBB
- Cols 9-22: B (black)
- Cols 23-25: Y (yellow corridor)
- Cols 26-27: OO (orange corridor)
- Cols 28-32: S (blue square) - wait, that's only 5 chars for SSSSS
- Actually: cols 28-32 would be SSSSS
Hmm, let me re-count more carefully:
Row 44: BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBYYYOOSSSSSBBBBBBBBBBBBBB
That's 44 characters for cols 9-52.
Let me count character by character:
B(9) B(10) B(11) B(12) B(13) B(14) B(15) B(16) B(17) B(18) B(19) B(20) B(21) B(22) B(23) B(24) B(25) B(26) Y(27) Y(28) Y(29) O(30) O(31) S(32) S(33) S(34) S(35) S(36) B(37) B(38) B(39) B(40) B(41) B(42) B(43) B(44) B(45) B(46) B(47) B(48) B(49) B(50) B(51) B(52)
Wait, that's 44 characters but I counted to col 52 which is 44-9+1 = 36... I'm getting confused.
Let me just use the segmentation data which says the right blue is at cols 34-38. So the right blue is at cols 34-38 in row 44.
Looking at the maze structure, I need to understand where the corridors lead. The goal might be to navigate the blue squares through the maze to specific destinations.
Let me think about this differently. The maze has two separate chambers (left and right) with corridors connecting them. The blue squares start at the bottom of each chamber and need to navigate through the corridors.
Looking at the maze:
- The left chamber has a yellow corridor that winds upward
- The right chamber has an orange corridor that winds upward
- The two corridors meet in the middle
The goal might be to get both blue squares to the top of the maze, or to a specific meeting point.
Currently:
- Left blue: rows 39-43, cols 19-23
- Right blue: rows 44-48, cols 34-38
The left blue is higher (rows 39-43) than the right blue (rows 44-48). To bring them to the same level, I need to move the right blue UP.
But when I tried UP, the left blue moved up but the right blue didn't (or vice versa). Let me check the movement pattern more carefully.
From the history:
- After UP (step 18): left=rows(39,43) cols(19,23), right=rows(44,48) cols(34,38)
- After UP (step 19): no change
So the left blue is stuck at rows 39-43 (can't move UP further), and the right blue is stuck at rows 44-48 (can't move UP further).
Wait, but earlier the left blue moved from rows 49-53 to rows 44-48 to rows 39-43. So it moved UP twice. And the right blue moved from rows 49-53 to rows 44-48. So it only moved UP once.
The issue is that the corridors are at different heights. The left blue's corridor goes higher than the right blue's corridor at this horizontal position.
Let me think about the maze structure again. Looking at the ascii:
- Row 39: BBBBBBBBBBSSSSSYYYYYYYYOOOOOOOOOOOOBBBBBBBBB
- Row 44: BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBYYYOOSSSSSBBBBBBBBBBBBBB
The left blue is at cols 19-23 in row 39. The corridor continues to the right (cols 24-31 are Y).
The right blue is at cols 34-38 in row 44. The corridor is to the left (cols 23-27 are Y and O).
So the left blue can move RIGHT (into the Y corridor), and the right blue can move LEFT (into the O corridor).
Let me try RIGHT to move the left blue right and the right blue left.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try RIGHT to move blues toward center
action(['RIGHT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
